findings.description: The North Carolina state seal image at the top of the letter appears in the PDF but is not present in the HTML rendering.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 has the NC state seal centered at top; HTML page 1 omits this seal image.

findings.description: Page 2 of the HTML combines the full content of PDF page 1 (the letter) with an embedded/nested image of the same letter, plus a re-typed version of the content, creating a triple-redundant representation of the same page.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 is a single letter. HTML page 2 shows the letter header/officials, then an embedded image of the full letter, then re-types the letter content below the image - all on one continuous page.

findings.description: The HTML rendering interleaves embedded images of pages with re-typed text versions, creating a confusing doubled reading order throughout pages 2-4.
findings.evidence: HTML pages 2-4 show embedded images of the PDF pages alongside re-typed text, causing content to appear twice in sequence.

findings.description: The DEQ logo/footer image at the bottom of PDF page 1 is rendered differently in HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF has the DEQ logo with contact information at the bottom; HTML shows it in a different format but information is preserved.

findings.description: The signature of David Miller on PDF page 3 is not visible in the HTML rendering of page 3.
findings.evidence: PDF page 3 shows a handwritten signature above 'David Miller / State Mining Engineer'; HTML shows the text 'for David Miller' without a signature image.

findings.description: The HTML rendering combines what are separate email chains and formal letters into a continuous flow with embedded images of the original pages alongside re-typed content, making it difficult to distinguish document boundaries.
findings.evidence: PDF pages 5-8 are distinct email chain pages; HTML pages 5-8 show both embedded images and re-typed text for the same emails.

findings.description: Social media icons (LinkedIn, Facebook, Twitter, Instagram) from Colonial Pipeline email signatures appear differently or inconsistently between PDF and HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF shows colored social media icons in email signatures; HTML shows them as colored circles or omits them.

findings.description: The Colonial Pipeline Company logo appears differently in scale/placement between PDF and HTML versions of the formal letter.
findings.evidence: PDF page 15 has the CD logo at top left; HTML renders it at different sizing but it is present.

findings.description: Rep. Dennis Riddell's NC state seal in email signature is referenced but may not render identically.
findings.evidence: PDF page 13 shows a small state seal image in the email signature; HTML version shows it differently.

findings.description: Mark Piazza's handwritten signature on the Colonial Pipeline letter is not reproduced in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF page 16 shows handwritten signature above 'Mark Piazza'; HTML shows only the typed name.

findings.description: The Attachment 1 document with detailed blasting requirements is shown both as embedded images and as re-typed text in the HTML, creating duplicated content.
findings.evidence: PDF pages 17-22 show the Attachment 1 document once; HTML shows it twice - as images and as re-typed text.

findings.description: Exhibit 1A site plan drawing is low quality in the PDF but present; the HTML includes both an embedded image version and a separate rendering.
findings.evidence: PDF page 23 has Exhibit 1A site plan; HTML shows it duplicated across pages.

findings.description: Multiple h1 elements used and heading hierarchy is inconsistent. The document uses h1 for 'Colonial Pipeline Company' title, but also has multiple h2 and h3 elements without a clear single-document hierarchy. The first h1 should be the document title, but a second h1 appears for the Colonial Pipeline Company header.
findings.evidence: HTML contains at least two h1 elements: one in the header section for Colonial Pipeline Company and one implied by the first-of-type CSS rule. Additionally, headings skip levels in places and the ATTACHMENT 1 section uses h2 then h3 without clear structure.

findings.description: Minor text variation in email signature block - Mark Piazza's mobile number formatting differs slightly.
findings.evidence: PDF shows 'm. 678.763 5911' and HTML shows 'm. 678.763 5911' - this is consistent, no issue on re-check.

findings.description: Several images have empty or generic alt text that could be more descriptive.
findings.evidence: Multiple images have alt='' (empty string) on pages 5 and 6 content, and several others throughout the attachment section. The State Seal image alt text is reasonable, but some map images could have more descriptive alt text.

findings.description: The HTML adds a blue color (#0078D7) styling note 'Thanks Brian. Colonial will provide the information to NCDEQ by noon tomorrow (eastern) as requested.' with a color attribution that isn't in the PDF, but the text itself is present in the PDF. No fabricated content detected.
findings.evidence: The text content matches page 3 of the PDF email thread. The styling is cosmetic only.

findings.description: No significant sections are missing. All major content sections from the 17-page PDF are represented in the HTML, including the formal letter (pages 1-2), email chain (pages 3-8), Colonial Pipeline formal letter (pages 9-10), Attachment 1 (pages 11-14), and Exhibits (pages 15-17).

Some empty/decorative image tags appear to be duplicated where the PDF had a single logo or decorative element, but these are structural artifacts of the page-by-page conversion rather than content duplication.
findings.evidence: Multiple img tags with empty alt text appear at the start of several main sections, corresponding to page-level decorative elements.
